nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, swampy ground and slow-moving freshwater across tropical West and Central Africa, from Senegal south to Angola and east to the Zambezi region of northern Zambia

decorPreferences: high lighting for red colouration and vein pattern
plant in groups of 5+ stems with spacing to keep lower leaves lit

summary: Guinea Ludwigia is a rare West African stem plant prized for its brick-red colouration and distinctive net-like vein pattern. It demands high light, pressurised CO2, soft acidic water, and heavy iron and micronutrient dosing to develop its full colour.
careNotes: Guinea Ludwigia (Ludwigia senegalensis) is a tropical stem plant in the Onagraceae family, native to marshes and slow-moving freshwater across West and Central Africa from Senegal south to Angola and east to the Zambezi region. In the hobby it is generally sold under the trade names 'Guinea Ludwigia' or Ludwigia sp. 'Guinea', often as tissue culture from specialty nurseries. Its defining feature is a striking metallic red-and-green reticulate (vein-like) leaf pattern that no other commonly traded Ludwigia produces, making it a sought-after accent plant in high-tech planted tanks. This is broadly considered an advanced plant, not suited to beginners. Soft water is strongly preferred, with kH generally cited in the 0-7 dKH range. The three variables that most determine how the plant looks are light intensity, CO2 concentration, and iron availability: high-intensity full-spectrum lighting combined with pressurised CO2 at 25-40 mg/L and heavy micronutrient dosing — at roughly double the manufacturer's standard rate, with iron maintained at around 0.01-0.5 mg/L — produces the brick-red colouration and pronounced vein contrast the plant is known for. Macronutrient levels of approximately 20 mg/L nitrate and 2-3 mg/L phosphate are also commonly recommended. Without adequate light, CO2, and iron, the plant will survive but shift toward pale green or orange tones and lose the vein definition that makes it distinctive. Plant in groups of five or more stems with enough spacing that lower leaves remain illuminated; dense packing tends to cause the lower portion of stems to lose their leaves. The plant is particularly fragile during the transition from its above-water (emersed) growth form to fully submerged growth — a phase that tissue culture plants go through after planting — so avoid rapid parameter swings during the first few weeks in the tank. Moderate water flow is generally considered beneficial.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: trim healthy tops from established stems and replant directly into nutrient-rich substrate. The cut mother stem will branch and produce lateral side shoots, allowing a dense red accent group to be built up from a small starter portion. Lateral shoots can also be separated once they have rooted.
